topic: What happens in your brain during a panic attack
headline: Your Brain Isn't Broken During Panic Attacks
imagePrompt: Cinematic medical illustration of a translucent human brain with the amygdala highlighted in soft amber glow, surrounded by neural pathways lighting up in gentle blue and white pulses, cross-section view showing limbic system structures, professional anatomical detail with soft dramatic lighting against deep navy background, photorealistic 3D rendering style
caption:
Here's what's actually happening when panic strikes:

Your amygdala (your brain's alarm system) detects a threat—real or perceived—and hits the emergency button. Within milliseconds, it bypasses your logical thinking brain and floods your body with stress hormones.

Your prefrontal cortex (the rational part) goes temporarily offline. That's why you can't "just calm down" or "think your way out of it."

Meanwhile, your sympathetic nervous system kicks into overdrive: heart racing, chest tight, breath shallow. Your brain genuinely believes you're in danger, even when you're sitting safely on your couch.

The good news? This is a normal protective mechanism gone into overdrive. Your brain isn't broken—it's doing exactly what it evolved to do, just with faulty alarm settings.

With the right techniques (breathwork, grounding, therapy), you can literally rewire these pathways over time.

Save this if you've ever felt like something was "wrong" with you during a panic attack. Nothing is broken. You're just human with a very sensitive alarm system.
